children.text: How humans produce and organize the sounds of their language into an abstract system of phonemes and syllables.

When we speak of the phonetics and phonology of a singing voice, we are talking about the way singers produce linguistic sounds (phonetics) and how those sounds are organized into an abstract system (phonology). That is, phonetics refers to the physical gestures and acoustic signals that make up speech, or what your tongue, lips, and vocal folds are doing to create a sound. Phonology, by contrast, is how those sounds are patterned and interpreted within a language. It is the system that allows listeners to recognize which differences in sound matter and which do not.
To transcribe and study these sounds, linguists use the

, a standardized system for representing speech sounds across languages. Each symbol corresponds to a specific sound, regardless of how that sound is spelled in any particular language. For example, the letter < c > in Spanish can be represented with a /k/ in the IPA in words like "
" or as a /s/ in words like "

. The IPA provides analysts with a uniform and precise code for indicating the gestures and place of articulation for linguistic sounds independent from the phonological systems in which they are locally organized.
The IPA organizes sounds first according to their

. That is, instead of us saying "an 's' sound," we can use a term like "alveolar fricative". "Alveolar" here is the place of articulation, because the tip of the tongue touches the alveolar ridge to make this sound. "Fricative" is the manner of articulation, which is when we allow a stream of high-frequency air to pass through the opening we've created with our tongue placement. In comparison, the sound /k/ is called a "velar stop," because the tongue touches the velum and stops the air for a brief moment. Below is a diagram of the
, which are the parts of the mouth, nose, and throat/vocal tract used to produce speech sounds.

is an organizing unit that indexes a meaningful contrast in a language. In other words, it is a sound that, if changed, can change the meaning of a word. For example, in Spanish /p/ vs /b/ distinguishes the words "
", or the sounds /β/ and /b/ distinguish the words "

". Phonemes are written between slashes / / when discussed in a general sense. However, the actual production of sound is written between brackets [ ]. That means /s/ refers to alveolar fricatives in general, whereas [s] describes a sound someone actually produced.
are the different ways a phoneme can be pronounced without changing meaning. Another term for allophone is a
as these are not different phones but different

For example, the phoneme /s/ in Spanish can be pronounced as a voiceless alveolar fricative /s/ or a voiced alveolar fricative /z/, and in some dialects it is pronounced as an aspiration /h/ or completely deleted /ø/. Allophones are written between brackets.
